vulnerabilities.title: MagicLpAggregator._getReserves Returns (0,0) Due to Missing Return Statement
vulnerabilities.summary: MagicLpAggregator._getReserves() declares local variables via tuple destructuring of pair.getReserves(), but uses unnamed return values and has no explicit return statement. The function always returns (0, 0). This causes latestAnswer() to compute `minAnswer * (0 + 0) / totalSupply = 0`, making any Cauldron using this oracle value all LP collateral at zero.

description.desc: Function declares local variables via tuple destructuring but has no return statement; unnamed return values default to (0, 0)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL code defect. The _getReserves() function is fundamentally broken - it discards the pair's reserve values and returns zeros. Any integration of MagicLpAggregator as a price oracle for LP tokens will produce a zero price, potentially enabling unlimited borrowing against worthless-valued collateral or causing mass liquidations depending on how exchangeRate=0 is interpreted by the consuming Cauldron. The function being `virtual` means subclasses could fix it, but the base implementation is defective.

vulnerabilities.title: FeeCollectable.setFeeParameters Validates Old State Instead of New Input
vulnerabilities.summary: setFeeParameters() checks `if (feeBips > BIPS)` instead of `if (_feeBips > BIPS)`. It validates the current state variable rather than the new input parameter. This allows setting feeBips to any uint16 value (up to 65535 = 655.35%) as long as the current value is valid. The first call always passes since initial feeBips is 0. Once set above BIPS, calculateFees() will cause underflow (userAmount = amountIn - feeAmount where feeAmount > amountIn) and subsequent setFeeParameters calls will permanen

description.desc: Validation checks `feeBips > BIPS` (state variable) instead of `_feeBips > BIPS` (input parameter)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any contract inheriting FeeCollectable that calls setFeeParameters with an invalid _feeBips value will: (1) permanently break calculateFees causing DoS for all fee-related operations, (2) permanently lock the invalid fee value since future setFeeParameters calls revert, and (3) require contract redeployment to fix. Even if the operator is trusted, an accidental misconfiguration (e.g., passing basis points as a percentage, like 5000 instead of 500) creates an irrecoverable state.

vulnerabilities.title: Router._validateDecimals Underflows When baseDecimals > quoteDecimals
vulnerabilities.summary: Router._validateDecimals performs `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` as uint8 subtraction. In Solidity 0.8+, this underflows and reverts when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals. This prevents creating any pool via Router where the base token has more decimals than the quote token, even if the absolute difference is within acceptable range (e.g., base=18, quote=8).

description.desc: quoteDecimals - baseDecimals underflows as uint8 when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The Router is the primary user-facing contract for pool creation. The defect blocks createPool for token pairs where base has more decimals than quote, and blocks createPoolETH with useTokenAsQuote=true for virtually all tokens. Users can work around this by calling Factory.create directly or swapping the base/quote order, but this degrades UX and may confuse integrators.

vulnerabilities.title: DegenBox.transferMultiple Only Validates First Recipient Address
vulnerabilities.summary: DegenBox.transferMultiple() checks `require(tos[0] != address(0))` but does not validate subsequent recipients. Shares transferred to address(0) at indices > 0 are permanently locked, as no mechanism exists to recover them from the zero address balance.

description.desc: Only tos[0] is checked for zero address; tos[1..n] can be address(0)

vulnerabilities.impact: Shares sent to address(0) via transferMultiple at indices > 0 are permanently locked. There is no mechanism in DegenBox to recover shares from address(0) — withdraw requires the `allowed(from)` modifier which cannot be satisfied for address(0) (no masterContract can approve for address(0), and msg.sender cannot be address(0)). While the most likely trigger scenario involves a UI bug or crafted cook call, the code defect is clear: the validation is incomplete compared to the single `transfer` function's pattern.

vulnerabilities.title: CauldronV4 Blacklist Becomes Stale After BentoBox Ownership Transfer
vulnerabilities.summary: CauldronV4 constructor and init() blacklist the current BentoBox owner: `blacklistedCallees[Owned(address(bentoBox)).owner()] = true`. This captures the owner address at deployment time. If BentoBox ownership is later transferred, the new owner is NOT blacklisted. An attacker could use ACTION_CALL in cook() to call the new BentoBox owner address, which may have special privileges (e.g., if the owner is a multisig or governance contract with executor capabilities).

description.desc: blacklistedCallees[Owned(address(bentoBox)).owner()] = true set only at init; not updated on ownership changes

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The blacklist mechanism intended to protect BentoBox-privileged addresses becomes stale after ownership transfer. While the masterContract owner can manually fix this via setBlacklistedCallee(), the gap between ownership transfer and manual blacklisting creates a window of vulnerability. The actual exploitability depends on the new owner's contract interface, but the security invariant (BentoBox owner should always be blacklisted) is violated.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP Uses tx.origin for Fee Rate Determination Instead of msg.sender
vulnerabilities.summary: MagicLP.sellBase(), sellQuote(), and flashLoan() pass tx.origin to querySellBase/querySellQuote for fee rate calculation. This means fees are determined by the EOA that initiated the transaction, not the actual caller. Smart contract wallets, aggregators, and meta-transaction relayers will get fees based on the EOA rather than the trading entity, breaking fee model isolation.

description.desc: querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender for fee lookup

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is MEDIUM. The defect breaks the fee model's ability to differentiate rates based on the actual trading entity. While the current FeeRateModelImpl may not differentiate rates (source unavailable), the interface is designed for it, and the implementation can be upgraded. The defect also breaks compatibility with smart contract wallets and account abstraction (ERC-4337), which is increasingly important. LP providers may receive incorrect fee revenue if the fee model is ever updated to use trader-dependent rates.

vulnerabilities.title: LockingMultiRewards withdraw()/getRewards() lack nonReentrant and transfer tokens to msg.sender enabling callback-based multi-claim/lock inconsistencies
vulnerabilities.summary: LockingMultiRewards performs external token transfers in withdraw() (stakingToken.safeTransfer) and in _getRewards() (rewardToken.safeTransfer) without a reentrancy guard. Although state is generally updated before transfers, the contract has multiple interacting accounting domains (balances, supplies, reward locks, reward accrual) that could be re-entered to exploit ordering across functions (e.g., re-enter getRewards/withdraw/lock/stake) to mint/claim rewards twice, bypass reward lock timing,

description.desc: external token transfers in withdraw() and _getRewards() without nonReentrant; multiple state domains updated around those calls

vulnerabilities.impact: Financial impact is the time-value bypass of reward locks - users get rewards immediately instead of waiting an epoch (typically 7 days). If combined with a flash loan or large stake manipulation, an attacker could earn and immediately claim significant rewards without the intended lockup period. Additionally, the stakingTokenBalance desync in withdraw() temporarily breaks the recover() function's skim check invariant. The code defect is real regardless of current token configurations because the contract accepts arbitrary reward tokens.

vulnerabilities.title: cook() Solvency Check Bypass via Unhandled Action Codes
vulnerabilities.summary: The cook() function's solvency check can be entirely bypassed by appending an unhandled action code (e.g., ACTION_ACCRUE=8, or any value like 0, 1, 3, 9) after ACTION_BORROW or ACTION_REMOVE_COLLATERAL. The unhandled action falls to the else branch calling _additionalCookAction(), which returns a default-initialized CookStatus{needsSolvencyCheck:false, hasAccrued:false}. The line `status = returnStatus` overwrites the previously set needsSolvencyCheck=true, causing the final solvency check to be

description.desc: else branch calls _additionalCookAction which returns default CookStatus; status = returnStatus overwrites needsSolvencyCheck=true set by ACTION_BORROW

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. The solvency check is the sole mechanism preventing undercollateralized borrowing in Cauldrons. Its complete bypass allows an attacker to borrow the entire MIM balance of any CauldronV4 or BlastCauldronV4 clone with minimal or zero effective collateral. This would create massive bad debt and drain protocol funds. All deployed CauldronV4 clones and BlastCauldronV4 clones are affected.

vulnerabilities.title: BlastOnboardingBoot._claimable Treats MIM and USDB as Equal Value
vulnerabilities.summary: _claimable() computes user pool shares by summing raw MIM and USDB locked amounts: totalLocked = totals[MIM].locked + totals[USDB].locked. Since MIM and USDB have different values (I=0.998, meaning 1 MIM ≈ 0.998 USDB), depositing the less valuable token gives disproportionate shares. If MIM depegs further, an attacker buying cheap MIM and depositing it gets the same claim weight as expensive USDB deposits.

description.desc: totalLocked = totals[MIM].locked + totals[USDB].locked sums raw amounts of different-valued tokens

vulnerabilities.impact: At the baseline I=0.998, the impact is approximately 0.2% dilution for USDB depositors. However, if MIM depegs further (which has historical precedent for algorithmic stablecoins), the impact scales linearly. At 1 MIM = 0.9 USDB, USDB depositors lose approximately 5-10% of their fair share depending on the MIM/USDB deposit ratio. The total value at risk is proportional to the total USDB locked multiplied by the dilution percentage. This is a genuine code defect because the protocol explicitly acknowledges the value difference (I=0.998) but fails to account for it in share distribution.

vulnerabilities.title: Router AddLiquidity Pulls Unintended Excess Tokens from User
vulnerabilities.summary: The Router's `addLiquidity` function can pull significantly more tokens from a user's wallet than they specified, draining their funds if they have granted infinite approval.

description.desc: baseInAmount incorporates pool's excess balance, which is then used to pull tokens from msg.sender

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The defect causes the Router to pull more tokens from users than they specified when LP pools have excess balance. This affects addLiquidity, addLiquidityETH, and previewAddLiquidity. Impact: (1) Users with infinite/large approvals lose funds equal to the pool's excess balance. (2) The previewAddLiquidity view function gives incorrect previews, misleading UIs. (3) Transactions may unexpectedly revert if user balance is insufficient for the inflated amount. (4) An attacker can weaponize this via front-running with token donations.

vulnerabilities.title: Strategy Yield Sandwich Attack via Public Harvest
vulnerabilities.summary: An attacker can extract accumulated strategy yield by flash-loaning tokens, depositing them into DegenBox to gain a massive share of the pool, calling the public `harvest` function to realize the yield, and immediately withdrawing their shares for a profit.

description.desc: Public harvest function updates totals[token].elastic without anti-sandwich protections

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH. All DegenBox depositors with active strategies are affected. The attacker captures a portion of accumulated yield proportional to their deposited share. With sufficient flash loan capital (which is freely available), the attacker can capture nearly 100% of all pending strategy yield. This represents ongoing theft of yield from legitimate depositors every time a strategy accumulates unrealized profit.

vulnerabilities.title: Router Incompatible with Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: The Router's `removeLiquidityETH` function assumes the exact amount of tokens returned by `sellShares` is received, causing it to revert if the token implements a fee-on-transfer mechanism.

description.desc: token.safeTransfer assumes exact amount was received from sellShares

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect causes a permanent DoS for the removeLiquidityETH function when used with FOT token pools. Users can work around this by calling removeLiquidity directly (which doesn't route through the Router as intermediary) and then unwrapping WETH manually. The Factory allows permissionless pool creation, so FOT token pools can exist. Similar issues may affect swapTokensForETH and swapETHForTokens for FOT tokens in multi-hop paths, though single-hop swaps where the FOT token goes directly to the user are not affected.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP fee model can be abused to revert or overcharge swaps by returning extreme fee rates
vulnerabilities.summary: MagicLP queries an external IFeeRateModel for (lpFeeRate, mtFeeRate) and subtracts both from the computed output. If the fee model returns a combined fee >= 100% of output, subtraction underflows and reverts, halting swaps/flashloan settlement. Even if it doesn't underflow, extreme fees can effectively confiscate output or route it to the maintainer.

description.desc: _MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_.getFeeRate(trader, _LP_FEE_RATE_); receiveAmount = receiveAmount - lpFee - mtFee; // no bounds check on returned rates
Affected function: create

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. All MagicLP pools sharing the same FeeRateModel would be affected simultaneously. Swaps (sellBase, sellQuote), flash loans, and any Router operations that call these functions would all revert or lose funds to the maintainer. This impacts core AMM functionality. Recovery requires the FeeRateModel owner to fix the implementation, or users must wait for pool migration (each pool's _MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_ is immutable after init).

vulnerabilities.title: Unclearable Bad Debt Causes Infinite Interest Accrual
vulnerabilities.summary: In liquidate(), if a user's position is heavily underwater, liquidating their full debt requires more collateral than they possess, causing an underflow revert. Liquidators must partially liquidate until collateral reaches zero. The remaining bad debt cannot be cleared because any further liquidation attempts will revert when subtracting from zero collateral.

description.desc: Underflow revert when required collateralShare exceeds userCollateralShare

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact. All borrowers in the Cauldron are affected because interest on bad debt inflates totalBorrow.elastic, increasing the actual MIM amount each borrowPart represents. Over time, this creates a growing discrepancy between what healthy borrowers owe and what they originally borrowed. Protocol fees (feesEarned) also inflate as they accrue on uncollectable debt. Recovery requires admin intervention (e.g., contract upgrade or external debt repayment) since no on-chain mechanism exists to write off bad debt.

vulnerabilities.title: Fee Rate Manipulation via tx.origin in MagicLP
vulnerabilities.summary: The sellBase and sellQuote functions pass tx.origin to the fee rate model instead of msg.sender. This means the fee rate is determined by the EOA that initiated the transaction, not the calling contract.

description.desc: Use of tx.origin for authorization/fee calculation

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The code defect is real and affects three critical functions (sellBase, sellQuote, flashLoan). Impact includes: (1) Incompatibility with smart contract wallets and account abstraction, where the EOA relayer's fee rate is applied instead of the wallet's; (2) If FeeRateModel differentiates by address, enables phishing attacks to exploit VIP fee rates; (3) Protocol fee revenue may be incorrect if fee model returns different rates for EOAs vs contract addresses. The defect exists regardless of the current FeeRateModel implementation since it's an architectural flaw.

vulnerabilities.title: Inconsistent Units in CauldronV4 Borrow Limits
vulnerabilities.summary: The borrow limit check compares cap.total against totalBorrow.elastic (which includes accrued interest) but compares cap.borrowPartPerAddress against newBorrowPart (which is in parts, excluding interest).

description.desc: Inconsistent comparison units (elastic vs base/parts)

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect allows per-user borrowing to exceed intended MIM limits over time as interest accrues. This is a risk management bypass rather than a direct fund extraction vulnerability. The impact increases with time and interest rate. The admin can periodically adjust the limits to mitigate, but the inconsistency means the limits don't behave uniformly. The global limit becoming stricter over time may also prevent legitimate new borrows.

vulnerabilities.title: DegenBox.transferMultiple only checks tos[0] != 0, enabling silent burning/locking of shares to address(0) for later indices
vulnerabilities.summary: DegenBox.transferMultiple validates only the first recipient is non-zero, but subsequent recipients can be address(0). Shares transferred to address(0) are effectively burned/locked without reducing totals.base, breaking share ownership distribution expectations and potentially harming users if used by integrators that assume safety checks.

description.desc: require(tos[0] != address(0)) but loop does not validate tos[i] for i>0

vulnerabilities.impact: The direct impact is permanent loss of shares for any transfer where tos[i>0] == address(0). While self-inflicted calls are unlikely, the risk increases when integrating contracts (like CauldronV4's cook function with ACTION_BENTO_TRANSFER_MULTIPLE) pass through user-controlled arrays. The invariant INV-STATE-008 (all recipients must be non-zero) is directly violated. Shares stranded at address(0) cannot be recovered, and totals.base is not adjusted, so the shares effectively become phantom supply.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLpAggregator Vulnerable to Flash Loan Spot Price Manipulation
vulnerabilities.summary: The `MagicLpAggregator` calculates the LP token price using the spot reserves of the MagicLP pool: `minAnswer * (baseReserve + quoteReserve) / pair.totalSupply()`. An attacker can use a flash loan to heavily imbalance the pool (e.g., swapping a massive amount of base tokens for quote tokens). In a constant-product-like AMM, imbalancing the pool significantly increases the sum of the reserves (`baseReserve + quoteReserve`). This artificially inflates the LP token price reported by the oracle, all

description.desc: LP price calculated using spot (baseReserve + quoteReserve)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: Any Cauldron using MagicLpAggregator as its oracle (once _getReserves is correctly implemented) would be vulnerable to complete draining of available MIM. The attacker can inflate LP collateral value by 3-5x or more in a single atomic transaction, borrow the maximum MIM possible, and leave the protocol with unrecoverable bad debt. All MIM lenders in the affected Cauldron lose funds.

vulnerabilities.title: Unprotected ETH Wrapping Allows Skimming of Stuck ETH in DegenBox
vulnerabilities.summary: In `DegenBox.deposit`, when `token_ == USE_ETHEREUM`, the contract calls `IWETH.deposit{value: amount}()`. It does not verify that `amount <= msg.value`. Furthermore, the skim check (`amount <= _tokenBalanceOf(token).sub(total.elastic)`) is explicitly bypassed for `USE_ETHEREUM`. This allows any user to call `deposit` with `msg.value = 0` and `amount` equal to the DegenBox's raw ETH balance. The contract will wrap its own stuck ETH into WETH and credit the corresponding shares to the attacker's

description.desc: IWETH.deposit{value: amount}() called without checking amount <= msg.value

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The impact is limited to stuck/accidentally-sent ETH in the DegenBox contract. On Blast, the contract may accumulate ETH from native yield or gas refunds more easily. The vulnerability allows theft of these accumulated funds. While the amount at risk is variable and may be small, the defect is clear: the contract wraps its own ETH without validating msg.value.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP sellShares Target Rounding Asymmetry Creates Cumulative Pricing Drift
vulnerabilities.summary: In MagicLP.sellShares(), token amounts returned to the user use floor division (round down), while _BASE_TARGET_ and _QUOTE_TARGET_ reductions use divCeil (round up). This asymmetry means targets decrease faster than proportional to the actual tokens removed, accumulating error over many withdrawals. Over time, targets drift significantly below their correct values, distorting PMM pricing curves.

description.desc: baseAmount uses floor division (baseBalance * shareAmount / totalShares) but _BASE_TARGET_ uses divCeil: uint112(uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) - (uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * shareAmount).divCeil(totalShares))

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW practical impact but CONFIRMED code defect. Each operation creates at most 1 wei error per target. For the drift to materially affect PMM pricing (which uses targets as equilibrium reference points), millions of operations would be needed for 18-decimal tokens. The defect is more concerning for low-decimal tokens where each wei represents more value. The primary risk is long-term pricing drift in heavily-used pools, not acute exploitation.

vulnerabilities.title: Factory.create Uses tx.origin for Deterministic Salt Preventing Contract-Initiated Pool Creation
vulnerabilities.summary: Factory.create() uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender to compute the deterministic clone salt. This means: (1) smart contract wallets and multisigs cannot reliably create pools since the deployer address varies, (2) the same EOA cannot create the same pool through different contracts, and (3) a pool's deterministic address depends on the EOA, not the calling contract. This breaks composability and prevents protocol-to-protocol integrations from deploying pools.

description.desc: address creator = tx.origin; bytes32 salt = _computeSalt(creator, ...) — tx.origin instead of msg.sender

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect breaks composability guarantees expected by smart contract callers. While the bootstrap flow works because it stores the pool address, the broader Factory functionality is impaired for programmatic use. The userPools registry is incorrect for any pool created through a contract intermediary (which is all pools, since Router is the expected entry point). The IFactory interface mismatch is an additional defect that prevents external contracts from predicting pool addresses.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP ratioSync Division by Zero When Reserve Is Zero
vulnerabilities.summary: MagicLP.ratioSync() adjusts targets proportionally to balance changes: _BASE_TARGET_ = uint112((uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * baseBalance) / uint256(_BASE_RESERVE_)). If _BASE_RESERVE_ is 0, this causes a division by zero revert. The implementation owner cannot recover the pool via ratioSync if a reserve is zero, compounding the buyShares DoS described in ECON-H004.

description.desc: _BASE_TARGET_ = uint112((uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * baseBalance) / uint256(_BASE_RESERVE_)); divides by _BASE_RESERVE_ with no zero check

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect is real but impact is limited: (1) ratioSync is admin-only (onlyImplementationOwner), (2) setParameters serves as an alternative recovery mechanism that does work with zero reserves since _resetTargetAndReserve reads balances directly, (3) reaching zero-reserve state requires admin action or extreme edge cases. The defect removes a recovery option but doesn't cause fund loss.

vulnerabilities.title: Router.createPoolETH uses safeTransferFrom for WETH held by Router, causing pool creation to revert and potentially strand user ETH expectations
vulnerabilities.summary: In createPoolETH(), the router wraps ETH into WETH, then attempts to move WETH to the clone using `address(weth).safeTransferFrom(address(this), clone, msg.value)`. Since Router has not approved itself, the WETH `transferFrom` will generally fail. This makes createPoolETH unusable and can break bootstrapping/liquidity flows that rely on it (especially during operational launches).

description.desc: createPoolETH(): weth.deposit{value: msg.value}(); then `address(weth).safeTransferFrom(address(this), clone, msg.value)` (should be safeTransfer)

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect is confirmed: safeTransferFrom is used where safeTransfer is correct. The severity of impact depends on the Blast WETH implementation. If WETH9-compatible (likely), the function works despite the bug. If standard ERC20, createPoolETH is completely broken and ETH-based pool creation through the Router is impossible. Either way, the code is objectively wrong and inconsistent with the rest of the Router.

vulnerabilities.title: BlastOnboarding.proxy bootstrapper unset or set to EOA can brick delegated functionality and confuse user flows
vulnerabilities.summary: BlastOnboarding is an OZ Proxy that delegates to `bootstrapper`. If bootstrapper is unset (address(0)) or set incorrectly (non-contract/EOA), any fallback-delegated calls will fail. While deposit/withdraw are implemented directly on the proxy contract, the bootstrapping/claim lifecycle relies on upgrading bootstrapper to BlastOnboardingBoot. Mis-setting bootstrapper can stall claiming or bootstrapping at critical times.

description.desc: _implementation() returns address(bootstrapper) with no contract-code check; receive() reverts; proxy delegates for unknown selectors

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium impact. The missing validation is a genuine code defect - proxy implementation setters should validate the target address. If exploited through misconfiguration: (1) Users cannot claim their bootstrapped LP shares, (2) The bootstrap() function cannot execute, (3) Calls silently succeed rather than reverting, making the issue harder to detect. User funds deposited during onboarding are not at direct risk (deposit/withdraw are implemented directly on the proxy), but the transition to bootstrapping and claiming is completely broken. Recovery requires an owner transaction to correctly set the bootstrapper.

vulnerabilities.title: Router._validateDecimals underflows when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals, blocking valid pool creation
vulnerabilities.summary: The _validateDecimals function performs `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` using uint8 arithmetic. In Solidity 0.8+, this underflows and reverts with a panic when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals. This blocks Router.createPool and createPoolETH for all valid token pairs where the base token has more decimals than the quote token (e.g., 18-decimal base with 6-decimal quote).

description.desc: uint8 subtraction `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` underflows when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals in Solidity 0.8+

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity for protocol functionality. The Router is the primary user-facing contract for pool creation. A significant class of valid token pairs (any pair where base decimals exceed quote decimals) cannot be created through the Router. This includes very common DeFi pairs like ETH/USDC or ETH/USDT where the base has 18 decimals and the quote has 6. While Factory.create provides a bypass, it lacks the Router's convenience features (bundled token transfers and initial liquidity provision in a single transaction).

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP.sellBase/sellQuote use tx.origin for fee rate lookup enabling fee manipulation
vulnerabilities.summary: sellBase and sellQuote pass tx.origin to querySellBase/querySellQuote for fee rate determination. If the FeeRateModel implementation discriminates by trader address, any contract calling these functions inherits the EOA's fee rate, not its own. This breaks composability and enables fee rate manipulation through intermediary contracts.

description.desc: querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender for fee rate lookup

vulnerabilities.impact: Currently LOW impact due to the non-discriminating FeeRateModelImpl. If the fee model is changed to discriminate by trader address: MEDIUM impact - reduced protocol fee revenue, unfair fee distribution, and broken composability for smart contract wallets. The defect is architectural and affects all pools using the same FeeRateModel.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP.buyShares allows imbalanced deposits that create reserve-target drift exploitable via arbitrage
vulnerabilities.summary: When buyShares receives imbalanced deposits (baseInputRatio != quoteInputRatio), shares are minted based on min(baseInputRatio, quoteInputRatio). Targets scale by this mintRatio, but reserves absorb the full deposit amounts. The excess tokens increase reserves without proportional target adjustment, creating a persistent drift between _BASE_RESERVE_ and _BASE_TARGET_ that distorts PMM pricing. An attacker can repeatedly deposit with excess tokens, then trade at mispriced rates.

description.desc: _BASE_TARGET_ scales by mintRatio but _setReserve uses full baseBalance; excess tokens absorbed without target correction

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM impact. The reserve-target drift is a real code defect that creates an inconsistent PMM state. The pricing distortion is proportional to the drift magnitude and the K parameter. LPs bear the cost through mispriced trades. The Router's addLiquidity mitigates this for normal users, but direct buyShares calls and addLiquidityUnsafe are vulnerable. The attack requires capital (for the imbalanced deposit) and the profitability depends on specific pool parameters. The defect accumulates over multiple imbalanced deposits.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP.sellShares divCeil target reduction creates cumulative rounding drift degrading LP value
vulnerabilities.summary: sellShares reduces _BASE_TARGET_ and _QUOTE_TARGET_ using divCeil (rounding UP the amount subtracted), while baseAmount/quoteAmount given to users use floor division (rounding DOWN). This asymmetric rounding means targets decrease faster than they mathematically should per share burned. Over many sellShares operations, targets systematically drift below their proportional values, eventually reaching 0 while reserves and totalSupply are still non-zero, breaking PMM pricing.

description.desc: _BASE_TARGET_ = uint112(uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) - (uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * shareAmount).divCeil(totalShares)); uses divCeil while baseAmount uses floor division

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW practical impact in isolation due to 1-wei-per-operation drift rate, but MEDIUM theoretical impact because: (1) the drift is irreversible and monotonic, (2) no minimum target enforcement exists, (3) if targets reach 0 or near-0, PMM pricing functions may revert or produce incorrect results (division by zero scenarios in pricing math), (4) remaining LPs absorb the cumulative rounding loss. Natural pool usage with many partial withdrawals over the pool's lifetime will create some drift, though likely immaterial for large pools.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP.setParameters truncates pool parameters to smaller types inconsistent with init()
vulnerabilities.summary: setParameters casts _LP_FEE_RATE_ to uint64, _K_ to uint64, and _I_ to uint128 before storing to uint256 state variables. While init() stores full uint256 values without truncation. Current MAX constants fit within these smaller types, but this creates an inconsistency: if MAX constants are ever increased, or if an attacker finds edge cases near the type boundaries, values could be silently truncated, corrupting pool pricing parameters.

description.desc: _LP_FEE_RATE_ = uint64(newLpFeeRate); _K_ = uint64(newK); _I_ = uint128(newI); truncation to smaller types before storing to uint256 state vars

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW to MEDIUM severity code defect. Zero current financial impact since all MAX values fit within their cast types. However: (1) the code is objectively inconsistent between init() and setParameters(), (2) it introduces a latent truncation risk if constants change, (3) it violates the principle that validated values should be stored exactly as validated. The defect is in the code's correctness, not in its current exploitability.

vulnerabilities.title: LockingMultiRewards withdraw/getRewards lack nonReentrant; reward token callbacks (ERC777-style) may reenter and corrupt accounting
vulnerabilities.summary: LockingMultiRewards performs external token transfers in withdraw() and _getRewards() without a reentrancy guard. If stakingToken or a rewardToken is an ERC777-like token (or otherwise has a callback), a malicious token contract could reenter and call withdraw/getRewards again mid-execution, potentially causing inconsistent updates to balances/supplies or double-claiming if state is not fully reset before transfers in all branches.

description.desc: external safeTransfer to msg.sender/user inside withdraw() and _getRewards() without nonReentrant

vulnerabilities.impact: The primary impact is bypassing the reward locking mechanism — rewards that should be locked until the next epoch are received immediately. This undermines the protocol's incentive design (the lock exists to prevent reward gaming). The financial impact is limited to the time-value of receiving rewards early (not double-claiming), though it could enable reward timing manipulation. The secondary impact in withdraw() is a transient invariant violation of stakingTokenBalance that has minimal practical consequences.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP ratioSync can permanently revert via division-by-zero when a reserve reaches 0, blocking admin recovery path and leaving pool in broken state
vulnerabilities.summary: ratioSync() rescales targets by multiplying by balance/reserve. If _BASE_RESERVE_ or _QUOTE_RESERVE_ is 0 while corresponding balance differs, the division reverts, preventing ratioSync from repairing target drift in precisely the scenarios where reserves may hit 0 (e.g., after administrative setParameters extraction or extreme imbalance).

description.desc: target rescale divides by _BASE_RESERVE_/_QUOTE_RESERVE_ without guarding reserve==0

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM impact. The ratioSync admin function becomes permanently unusable when a reserve is 0 and corresponding balance is non-zero. This is a genuine code defect (missing zero-check before division). However, the admin has an alternative recovery path through setParameters which calls _resetTargetAndReserve (no division). The pool is not permanently bricked, but an expected admin tool fails in precisely the edge case where it might be needed most.

vulnerabilities.title: LockingMultiRewards Operator-Only Lock Processing Freezes User Funds
vulnerabilities.summary: processExpiredLocks is gated by onlyOperators with no fallback mechanism for users to unlock their own expired positions. If the operator (Gelato automation) goes offline, loses credentials, or experiences extended downtime, all users with locked tokens cannot access their funds regardless of lock expiration.

description.desc: processExpiredLocks has onlyOperators modifier (line 397). No alternative user-callable unlock function exists in the contract.

vulnerabilities.impact: All users with locked positions are affected. The entire lockedSupply is at risk of being frozen if operator and owner become unavailable. Even with owner as fallback, there is an unnecessary centralization dependency. Users who lock tokens for 13 weeks have no self-service mechanism to reclaim their tokens after expiry. This is a design defect that violates the principle that users should be able to access their own funds after lock expiry without third-party intervention.

vulnerabilities.title: Reward Token Transfer Failure Permanently Bricks All User Reward Claims
vulnerabilities.summary: If any reward token becomes non-transferable (paused, blacklisted, or reverts on transfer), the _getRewards function permanently reverts for all affected users. Since reward tokens cannot be removed from the rewardTokens array, and _getRewards iterates all tokens in a single transaction, failure of one token blocks claiming of ALL reward tokens.

description.desc: _getRewards iterates all rewardTokens in single tx. safeTransfer at line 483 reverts if token is non-transferable. No try-catch, no skip mechanism, no removeReward function.

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. All users with non-zero locked reward amounts for the failing token lose access to ALL their accumulated rewards across every reward token. Staked tokens can still be withdrawn via withdraw(), but all reward value is permanently frozen. The impact scales with the total accumulated rewards in the contract and affects every staker who interacted with the system while the problematic token was distributing rewards.

vulnerabilities.title: notifyRewardAmount Near Epoch Boundary With Zero TotalSupply Permanently Loses Rewards
vulnerabilities.summary: When notifyRewardAmount is called with very small _remainingRewardTime (near epoch boundary) and minRemainingTime=0, the rewardRate becomes extremely high but is only distributed for a tiny time window. If totalSupply is 0 during this window (no stakers), all rewards are permanently unclaimable as rewardPerToken doesn't increase.

description.desc: rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime (line 380). If _remainingRewardTime=1, rate=amount/1. _rewardPerToken returns stored value unchanged when totalSupply_=0 (line 279).

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The entire notified reward amount for a period can be permanently lost. For a protocol distributing significant rewards weekly, this could mean loss of substantial value. The stranded tokens can only be recovered by the owner via recover(), redirecting rewards intended for stakers to the protocol owner. The defect is amplified near epoch boundaries where _remainingRewardTime approaches 0, but even moderate remaining times with zero totalSupply result in proportional reward loss.

vulnerabilities.title: Arbitrageurs Can Sweep Pool Excess Balances via Router Swaps
vulnerabilities.summary: If a MagicLP pool accumulates excess tokens (via direct transfers, airdrops, or rounding), any user can sweep these tokens by calling `sellBaseTokensForTokens` with a minimal input amount. The Router transfers the user's input to the pool, and `sellBase` calculates `baseInput = baseBalance - _BASE_RESERVE_`, which includes the pool's existing excess, giving the attacker the output for both their input and the excess tokens.

description.desc: baseInput is calculated dynamically from total balance, absorbing pre-existing excess
Affected function: sellBaseTokensForTokens

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM impact. Tokens accidentally sent to the pool or accumulated through rounding are extractable by any user. While this doesn't steal from LP reserves (which are tracked via _BASE_RESERVE_/_QUOTE_RESERVE_), it does mean: (1) accidental transfers are immediately lost to MEV, (2) any protocol attempting to donate/airdrop to the pool's LPs has those tokens extracted by arbitrageurs, (3) the sync() function which could absorb excess into reserves for LP benefit is effectively useless since MEV bots will extract excess first. The severity is medium because it requires excess to exist (not from normal operations) and doesn't affect tracked reserves.

vulnerabilities.title: Precision Loss in Reward Rate Calculation Permanently Locks Dust
vulnerabilities.summary: In `notifyRewardAmount`, the `rewardRate` is calculated via integer division (`amount / _remainingRewardTime`). This truncation means the actual distributed rewards over the epoch (`rewardRate * _remainingRewardTime`) will be slightly less than the notified `amount`. The remainder (dust) is never distributed and remains permanently locked in the contract.

description.desc: reward.rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime causes precision loss

vulnerabilities.impact: CONFIRMED but LOW severity in practice. The truncation is a real code defect where notified reward amounts are not fully distributed. Maximum dust per notification is bounded by _remainingRewardTime - 1 wei (up to 604799 wei for 7-day epochs). Over a year with 5 tokens, total accumulated dust is approximately 157 million wei (~0.000000157 tokens with 18 decimals). The owner's recover() function provides a mitigation path. This is a well-known limitation of Synthetix-style reward distribution contracts and is commonly accepted in the industry.

vulnerabilities.title: Spot Price Flash Loan Manipulation for Oracle Exploitation
vulnerabilities.summary: `getMidPrice()` calculates the spot price dynamically based on current `_BASE_RESERVE_` and `_QUOTE_RESERVE_`. Because MagicLP allows flash loans, an attacker can borrow a massive amount of one token, drastically altering the reserves and the resulting `RState`. This heavily skews the `getMidPrice()` output within the same transaction.

description.desc: getMidPrice calculates price directly from current state.B and state.Q without time-weighting
Affected function: getMidPrice

vulnerabilities.impact: If MagicLpAggregator uses getMidPrice() as documented, this enables: (1) LP token value inflation/deflation for Cauldron collateral manipulation, (2) Unfair liquidations of existing borrowers, (3) Over-borrowing against artificially inflated collateral values, (4) Bad debt creation in lending protocols. The severity depends entirely on the MagicLpAggregator implementation (not in scope) and the TVL in connected Cauldrons.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP Pool Permanently Bricked When Admin Drains Reserve to Zero via setParameters
vulnerabilities.summary: If setParameters is called with baseOutAmount or quoteOutAmount draining either reserve to zero, _resetTargetAndReserve sets the corresponding reserve and target to 0. With totalSupply > 0 but a zero reserve, buyShares becomes permanently non-functional (neither the totalSupply==0 nor the baseReserve>0&&quoteReserve>0 branch applies). Additionally, any swap involving the zeroed-out token calls PMMPricing with V0=0, which reverts with ErrIsZero. The pool is permanently bricked — no new deposits,

description.desc: setParameters allows arbitrary baseOutAmount/quoteOutAmount withdrawal; _resetTargetAndReserve sets reserves/targets to post-withdrawal balance which can be 0; Math._SolveQuadraticFunctionForTrade reverts with ErrIsZero when V0=0
Affected function: _SolveQuadraticFunctionForTrade

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL for affected pool: All LP holders' remaining assets in the pool cannot be supplemented with new liquidity. Swaps involving the zeroed token are permanently broken. sellShares still works for partial recovery (users get proportional share of remaining quote tokens, but 0 base tokens). The pool is effectively dead without explicit admin recovery action. If admin is malicious, this is a permanent denial of service combined with fund extraction.

vulnerabilities.title: Smart Contract Composability Broken by tx.origin in Factory Salt Computation
vulnerabilities.summary: Factory.create uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender to compute the CREATE2 salt and register the pool creator, breaking integrations with smart contract wallets and DAOs.

description.desc: address creator = tx.origin;

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. No direct financial loss, but breaks composability with a significant class of users (DAOs, multisigs, smart contract wallets like Safe). Pool creation through intermediary contracts attributes ownership to the wrong entity. CREATE2 collisions prevent legitimate pool deployments when the same EOA submits identical-parameter pool creation transactions through different contracts. This is a design defect that violates the expected behavior of msg.sender-based identity in Ethereum's composability model.

vulnerabilities.title: Reward rate manipulation via late-epoch notification
vulnerabilities.summary: In `LockingMultiRewards.sol`, if `notifyRewardAmount` is called with `minRemainingTime = 0` very close to the end of an epoch, `_remainingRewardTime` will be extremely small (e.g., 1 second). This causes `rewardRate` to spike massively. An operator could intentionally or accidentally distribute an entire week's worth of rewards in a single block, allowing MEV searchers or the operator themselves to capture all rewards by staking just before the notification.

description.desc: rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime; without enforcing a strict minimum duration if minRemainingTime is 0

vulnerabilities.impact: If exploited, the entire notified reward amount for that call could be captured by a single staker who times their stake. Legitimate long-term stakers would receive little to nothing from that reward notification. The severity is moderated by: (1) operator access control requirement, (2) reward locking for one epoch, and (3) no public mempool on Blast. However, the code defect is real - there should be a hardcoded minimum for _remainingRewardTime.

vulnerabilities.title: ETH Pool Creation Reverts on Strict WETH Implementations
vulnerabilities.summary: `Router.createPoolETH` calls `weth.safeTransferFrom(address(this), clone, msg.value)` without first approving the Router to spend its own WETH. While standard WETH9 bypasses the allowance check when `src == msg.sender`, stricter WETH implementations or L2 native equivalents might enforce the allowance check universally, causing ETH pool creation to always revert.

description.desc: `address(weth).safeTransferFrom(address(this), clone, msg.value);` without prior `approve`

vulnerabilities.impact: On chains with strict WETH implementations, createPoolETH is completely non-functional. Users cannot create ETH-paired pools through the Router. This is a DoS of a core protocol function. Even on WETH9-compatible chains, the code contains a latent defect (reliance on non-standard behavior) that makes cross-chain deployment fragile. The inconsistency with all other ETH functions in Router that correctly use safeTransfer confirms this is an unintentional bug.

vulnerabilities.title: Unregistered Rebasing Tokens Leak Yield via DegenBox Skimming
vulnerabilities.summary: If a rebasing token is enabled in BlastBox but not added to the BlastTokenRegistry, it defaults to AUTOMATIC yield mode. The accrued yield can be stolen by any user via the DegenBox `deposit` skimming feature.

description.desc: setTokenEnabled only configures CLAIMABLE mode if token is in registry, defaulting to AUTOMATIC and leaking yield
Affected function: deposit()

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect is real - setTokenEnabled lacks enforcement that rebasing tokens must be in the registry. If triggered, 100% of native Blast yield for the misconfigured token would be extractable by any user via the deposit skimming mechanism. The current deployment is safe because Blast.s.sol correctly registers WETH and USDB before enabling them. The risk is for future token additions where an admin might not follow the correct ordering.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP buyShares Permanently DoS'd When One Reserve Depleted to Zero
vulnerabilities.summary: In MagicLP.buyShares(), when totalSupply() > 0 but one reserve is zero (e.g., quoteReserve == 0), the code falls through both conditional branches: the initial supply branch (totalSupply == 0 is false) and the normal case branch (baseReserve > 0 && quoteReserve > 0 is false). The shares variable remains 0, causing _mint(to, 0) to revert with ErrMintAmountNotEnough. This permanently blocks new deposits into the pool.

description.desc: buyShares has only two branches for share calculation: totalSupply()==0 and (baseReserve > 0 && quoteReserve > 0); if totalSupply > 0 and one reserve is 0, shares stays 0 and _mint reverts

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is a DoS on the pool's deposit (buyShares) functionality when one reserve reaches zero. The dead shares (1001) make this condition sticky - totalSupply never reaches 0 naturally. Existing LPs can still exit via sellShares (their proportional share of the remaining reserve). Admin can recover the pool via setParameters, but this requires manual intervention and token injection. The severity is HIGH as it blocks pool operation, though it requires an unusual state to trigger and is recoverable by admin.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP setParameters Unsafe Narrowing Cast Silently Truncates Pool Parameters
vulnerabilities.summary: MagicLP.setParameters() stores _LP_FEE_RATE_ as uint64, _K_ as uint64, and _I_ as uint128 via explicit narrowing casts, despite the state variables being uint256. While current validation ranges fit within these types, the storage values after setParameters differ from after init() (which stores full uint256). If MAX_I, MAX_K, or MAX_LP_FEE_RATE constants are ever increased, values would be silently truncated without revert.

description.desc: _LP_FEE_RATE_ = uint64(newLpFeeRate); _K_ = uint64(newK); _I_ = uint128(newI); — narrowing cast on uint256 state variables

vulnerabilities.impact: Zero impact under current code. The narrowing casts are a code quality concern and maintenance risk, but not an active defect. The inconsistency between init() (full uint256 storage) and setParameters() (narrowed storage) has no observable effect since valid values fit within the narrowed types.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicLP.flashLoan allows fee-free flash loans when borrower returns both tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: In flashLoan, the fee validation only triggers in 'sell quote' (baseBalance < _BASE_RESERVE_) or 'sell base' (quoteBalance < _QUOTE_RESERVE_) branches. If the borrower returns both tokens in full (both balances >= reserves after callback), neither branch executes. The function succeeds with _sync() updating reserves, but zero mtFee is collected. An attacker gets free flash loan capital for external arbitrage.

description.desc: Neither sell_quote nor sell_base branch executes when both post-callback balances >= reserves; no fee collected in this path

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is loss of fee revenue for the maintainer and LPs. Every flash loan that returns both tokens costs the protocol expected fee income. While LPs don't lose principal (tokens are returned), they lose the fee income that should compensate them for providing flash loan liquidity. The maintainer receives zero mtFee. This is exploitable by any on-chain actor and can be repeated indefinitely. The economic impact scales with the size and frequency of flash loans and the availability of external arbitrage opportunities.

vulnerabilities.title: Permissionless sync() lets attackers desync reserves/targets via donation to manipulate pricing and extract value on subsequent swaps
vulnerabilities.summary: Because MagicLP.sync() is permissionless and sets reserves to current token balances, an attacker can strategically donate base/quote tokens to the pool, then call sync() to lock in those donated balances as official reserves without a corresponding target/RState adjustment. This can shift PMM state and pricing, potentially enabling profitable swaps against the distorted curve.

description.desc: permissionless sync()->_sync() sets _BASE_RESERVE_/_QUOTE_RESERVE_ to balanceOf values; no target/RState recomputation

vulnerabilities.impact:
The code defect creates stored state inconsistency violating INV-ECON-006 (target-reserve ordering). Impact is:

1. STORED STATE: _RState_ can be inconsistent with reserve-target relationships after donation+sync.
2. PRICING: In non-ONE states, effective targets shift via adjustedTarget, changing PMM output amounts for subsequent trades.
3. TWAP: getMidPrice during _twapUpdate reflects the distorted state, enabling TWAP accumulator manipulation.
4. LP VALUE: Donated tokens become pool assets, distributed proportionally to all LPs on withdrawal via sellShares (which uses actual balances, not reserves).

The financial impact is bounded by: (a) adjustedTarget partially compensates for the inconsistency, and (b) in R=ONE state (common equilibrium), pricing is unaffected. The severity is lower than initially hypothesized but the defect is real.

vulnerabilities.title: Router multi-hop swaps accept arbitrary path addresses; malicious contracts can steal intermediate tokens because no pool validation is performed
vulnerabilities.summary: Router swap functions only validate path length, not that each path[i] is a genuine MagicLP (or factory-registered) pool with the expected base/quote tokens. Because Router transfers tokens directly to path[0] and each hop sends output to the next address, a malicious contract placed in the path can accept tokens and return nothing or return a misleading amount, causing user loss.

description.desc: only checks path length; then transfers tokens to path[0] and calls IMagicLP(path[i]).sellBase/sellQuote without verifying codehash/Factory registry

vulnerabilities.impact: No real-world impact. Users cannot be harmed by other users through this pattern. Each caller controls their own path and their own funds. Setting minimumOut > 0 provides output protection, and setting it to 0 is the caller's explicit choice to accept any output.

vulnerabilities.title: Router removeLiquidity(ETH) lacks deadline enforcement, enabling execution after user’s intended validity window (stale pricing / stale assumptions)
vulnerabilities.summary: Router.removeLiquidity and Router.removeLiquidityETH accept a deadline parameter but do not apply ensureDeadline(deadline) modifier. While MagicLP.sellShares checks deadline internally, Router transfers LP shares from the user to itself first. If sellShares reverts, the whole transaction reverts so LP shares return, but the missing Router-level deadline check can still allow stale operations if downstream logic changes or if future modifications add non-reverting side effects pre-sellShares.

description.desc: deadline parameter present but function not annotated with ensureDeadline(deadline)

vulnerabilities.impact: Low practical impact currently due to downstream enforcement, but confirmed code defect per the inconsistency with the established pattern. This is a defensive programming gap that could become exploitable under code evolution or non-standard token interactions.

vulnerabilities.title: LockingMultiRewards notifyRewardAmount is vulnerable to fee-on-transfer reward tokens causing reward insolvency and permanent claim reverts
vulnerabilities.summary: notifyRewardAmount transfers `amount` from operator but uses `amount` (not actual received) to set rewardRate. With fee-on-transfer reward tokens, the contract receives less than `amount` but commits to distributing rewards as if full `amount` was received, eventually running out of reward tokens and reverting transfers in _getRewards for all users.

description.desc: rewardToken.safeTransferFrom(..., amount); rewardRate computed from `amount` without checking actual received

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH. If a fee-on-transfer reward token is added: (1) Reward insolvency gradually develops as rewards are distributed at an inflated rate. (2) Eventually safeTransfer reverts for that token in _getRewards. (3) Since all reward tokens are claimed in a single loop, ALL reward claiming is bricked (aligns with INV-STATE-003). (4) No built-in recovery mechanism exists - reward tokens cannot be removed. (5) The recover() function could extract tokens but would worsen insolvency, not fix it. (6) All stakers are affected. This is a code defect regardless of whether the current deployment uses such tokens.

vulnerabilities.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Breaks LockingMultiRewards Staking Accounting
vulnerabilities.summary: LockingMultiRewards._stakeFor transfers `amount` tokens via safeTransferFrom and then adds `amount` to stakingTokenBalance. If the staking token is fee-on-transfer, the actual received amount is less than `amount`, but stakingTokenBalance is inflated. Over time, stakingTokenBalance exceeds actual token balance, causing insolvency when later users try to withdraw.

description.desc: safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, this, amount) followed by stakingTokenBalance += amount without checking actual received amount

vulnerabilities.impact: Code-level defect: missing balance verification after token transfer. If the contract is deployed with or migrated to a fee-on-transfer staking token, all stakers would be affected. Last withdrawers would lose their entire staked balance as the contract becomes insolvent. Current deployment with MIM is not affected. The same pattern in notifyRewardAmount would also cause reward insolvency with fee-on-transfer reward tokens (currently ARB, which is standard ERC20).

vulnerabilities.title: Unsafe Liquidity Addition Results in Permanent Loss of Excess Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: The `addLiquidityUnsafe` function pulls the exact `baseInAmount` and `quoteInAmount` from the user without adjusting for the pool's current ratio. Because `buyShares` mints LP tokens based on the minimum of the two input ratios, any excess tokens provided by the user do not yield additional LP shares. These excess tokens are permanently absorbed into the pool and can be immediately swept by MEV bots.

description.desc: Pulls exact user amounts without calling _adjustAddLiquidity, leading to ratio mismatch in buyShares

vulnerabilities.impact: No code defect exists. The behavior is intentional and clearly communicated through the function naming convention. The safe alternative (addLiquidity) exists for users who need ratio adjustment. The minimumShares parameter provides slippage protection even in the unsafe variant. This matches standard DeFi AMM patterns (e.g., Uniswap V2 mint requires pre-calculated amounts).

vulnerabilities.title: LockingMultiRewards earned() Misrepresents Claimable Amount Due to Reward Lock Delay
vulnerabilities.summary: The earned() function returns the full accumulated reward amount for a user, but the _getRewards() mechanism locks newly claimed rewards until the next epoch. External integrations (lending protocols, portfolio trackers, vaults) using earned() to assess a user's claimable value will overestimate by including locked rewards not yet transferable. This creates economic mispricing in composability scenarios.

description.desc: earned() returns full accumulated rewards without deducting currently locked portion; _getRewards() locks all new claims for one epoch

vulnerabilities.impact: No direct impact within the contract itself. The hypothetical impact depends entirely on external protocols incorrectly interpreting earned() as immediately claimable value. The contract itself is solvent and correctly tracks all rewards and locks.
